content.text: Azure's analytics platform spans five stages of the modern data pipeline: ingestion (streaming and batch data capture), storage (data lake and warehouse), processing and transformation (ETL/ELT and real-time streaming), serving and querying (SQL data warehouse, KQL engines), and visualization (Power BI dashboards). Multiple Azure services operate at each stage; the right combination depends on data volume, latency requirements, team skills, and workload type.

attrs.alt: Azure analytics architecture: Ingest (Event Hubs, IoT Hub, ADF) → Store (ADLS Gen2, Blob) → Process (Databricks, Synapse Spark, Stream Analytics) → Serve (Synapse SQL Pool, Azure Data Explorer) → Visualize (Power BI, Grafana)

content.text: Azure Data Factory is the cloud-native ETL and data integration service. It orchestrates data movement and transformation across more than 90 built-in connectors (Azure services, on-premises databases, SaaS APIs, REST endpoints). A pipeline is a logical grouping of activities — Copy Activity (moves data), Data Flow (transforms data using a visual Spark-based engine), and control flow activities (ForEach, IfCondition, Execute Pipeline).

ADF is the standard tool for batch data ingestion pipelines: move data from an on-premises SQL Server, an SAP system, a REST API, or Amazon S3 into ADLS Gen2 on a schedule, then trigger downstream Databricks or Synapse notebooks to process it.

# Create an Azure Data Factory
az datafactory factory create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --location eastus

# Create a linked service (connection to Azure Blob Storage)
az datafactory linked-service create \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --linked-service-name AzureBlobStorage \
  --properties '{
    "type": "AzureBlobStorage",
    "typeProperties": {
      "connectionString": "DefaultEndpointsProtocol=https;AccountName=mystorageaccount;AccountKey=<key>"
    }
  }'

# Create a pipeline with a Copy Activity (source: HTTP REST API → sink: ADLS Gen2)
az datafactory pipeline create \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name ingestFromAPI \
  --pipeline '{
    "activities": [
      {
        "name": "CopyFromAPI",
        "type": "Copy",
        "typeProperties": {
          "source": {"type": "RestSource"},
          "sink": {"type": "AzureBlobFSSink"}
        },
        "inputs": [{"referenceName": "RESTDataset", "type": "DatasetReference"}],
        "outputs": [{"referenceName": "ADLSDataset", "type": "DatasetReference"}]
      }
    ]
  }'

# Trigger a pipeline run immediately
az datafactory pipeline create-run \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name ingestFromAPI

# List recent pipeline runs
az datafactory pipeline-run query-by-factory \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --last-updated-after "2026-06-01T00:00:00Z" \
  --last-updated-before "2026-06-30T23:59:59Z" \
  --query "value[].{pipeline:pipelineName, status:status, duration:durationInMs, runId:runId}" \
  --output table

# Create a trigger (schedule: daily at 2 AM UTC)
az datafactory trigger create \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --trigger-name dailyIngest \
  --properties '{
    "type": "ScheduleTrigger",
    "typeProperties": {
      "recurrence": {
        "frequency": "Day",
        "interval": 1,
        "startTime": "2026-07-01T02:00:00Z",
        "timeZone": "UTC"
      }
    },
    "pipelines": [{"pipelineReference": {"referenceName": "ingestFromAPI", "type": "PipelineReference"}}]
  }'

az datafactory trigger start \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --trigger-name dailyIngest

Azure Event Hubs is a fully managed big-data streaming platform capable of ingesting millions of events per second. It is the standard Azure service for real-time telemetry ingestion, log streaming, change data capture (CDC), and clickstream data. Event Hubs is compatible with the Apache Kafka protocol — applications using the Kafka producer and consumer APIs can connect to Event Hubs without code changes, simply by pointing to the Event Hubs endpoint.

An Event Hub namespace holds one or more event hubs (equivalent to Kafka topics). Each event hub is divided into partitions — the unit of parallelism. Consumers in a consumer group each read from a distinct set of partitions, enabling parallel processing. Messages are retained for 1 to 90 days (depending on tier and configuration).
1 MB/s ingest per TU, up to 20 TU
1 MB/s per PU, up to 16 PU

# Create an Event Hubs namespace (Standard tier, 2 throughput units)
az eventhubs namespace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEventHubsNS \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard \
  --capacity 2 \
  --enable-kafka true

# Create an event hub with 32 partitions and 7-day retention
az eventhubs eventhub create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myEventHubsNS \
  --name telemetry \
  --partition-count 32 \
  --message-retention 7

# Enable Capture: auto-save events to ADLS Gen2 as Avro files
az eventhubs eventhub update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myEventHubsNS \
  --name telemetry \
  --enable-capture true \
  --capture-interval 300 \
  --capture-size-limit 314572800 \
  --destination-name EventHubArchive.AzureBlockBlob \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --blob-container telemetry-archive \
  --archive-name-format "{Namespace}/{EventHub}/{PartitionId}/{Year}/{Month}/{Day}/{Hour}/{Minute}/{Second}"

# Get the connection string for a consumer group
az eventhubs namespace authorization-rule keys list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myEventHubsNS \
  --name RootManageSharedAccessKey \
  --query primaryConnectionString \
  --output tsv

# List all event hubs and their partition counts
az eventhubs eventhub list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myEventHubsNS \
  --query "[].{name:name, partitions:partitionCount, retention:messageRetentionInDays}" \
  --output table

Azure Stream Analytics is a fully managed real-time analytics service. It reads events from Event Hubs, IoT Hub, or Blob Storage; runs SQL-like queries that apply windowed aggregations, joins, and pattern detection; and writes results to output sinks (Event Hubs, Azure SQL Database, Cosmos DB, Power BI, ADLS Gen2, Blob Storage). Stream Analytics is designed for event-driven pipelines where low latency (seconds, not minutes) is required.

# Create a Stream Analytics job
az stream-analytics job create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --job-name myStreamJob \
  --location eastus \
  --sku-name Standard \
  --output-error-policy Drop

# Add an input (read from Event Hub)
az stream-analytics input create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --job-name myStreamJob \
  --input-name telemetryInput \
  --properties '{
    "type": "Stream",
    "datasource": {
      "type": "Microsoft.EventHub/EventHub",
      "properties": {
        "eventHubName": "telemetry",
        "serviceBusNamespace": "myEventHubsNS",
        "sharedAccessPolicyName": "RootManageSharedAccessKey",
        "sharedAccessPolicyKey": "<key>"
      }
    },
    "serialization": {"type": "Json", "properties": {"encoding": "UTF8"}}
  }'

# Add output (write to Azure SQL Database)
az stream-analytics output create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --job-name myStreamJob \
  --output-name sqlOutput \
  --properties '{
    "datasource": {
      "type": "Microsoft.Sql/Server/Database",
      "properties": {
        "server": "mysqlserver.database.windows.net",
        "database": "mydb",
        "table": "telemetry_agg",
        "user": "sqladmin",
        "password": "<password>"
      }
    }
  }'

# Define the transformation query (5-minute tumbling window aggregation)
az stream-analytics transformation create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --job-name myStreamJob \
  --transformation-name main \
  --streaming-units 3 \
  --query-text "
    SELECT
      deviceId,
      AVG(temperature) AS avg_temp,
      MAX(temperature) AS max_temp,
      COUNT(*) AS event_count,
      System.Timestamp() AS window_end
    INTO sqlOutput
    FROM telemetryInput TIMESTAMP BY eventTime
    GROUP BY deviceId, TumblingWindow(minute, 5)
  "

# Start the job
az stream-analytics job start \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --job-name myStreamJob \
  --output-start-mode JobStartTime

Azure Synapse Analytics is an integrated analytics platform that combines a serverless SQL engine (Synapse Serverless SQL Pool — query files in ADLS Gen2 using SQL without loading data), a dedicated SQL data warehouse (Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool — MPP columnar database for complex analytical queries), and Apache Spark pools (Synapse Spark — for data engineering and ML), all accessible from a single interface called Synapse Studio.
Query ADLS Gen2 files (Parquet, CSV, JSON, Delta) with SQL
MPP columnar SQL engine, distributed storage
Per DWU (Data Warehouse Unit) per hour
Enterprise BI, predictable query performance, large aggregations
Apache Spark cluster with Python, Scala, R support
Data engineering (ETL), ML feature engineering, Delta Lake
KQL engine for time-series and log analytics
Data movement and transformation orchestration within Synapse

# Create a Synapse workspace
az synapse workspace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySynapseWS \
  --location eastus \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --file-system synapse \
  --sql-admin-login sqladmin \
  --sql-admin-login-password "SecureP@ss1!"

# Allow your IP to connect to Synapse (firewall rule)
az synapse workspace firewall-rule create \
  --workspace-name mySynapseWS \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name AllowMyIP \
  --start-ip-address 203.0.113.5 \
  --end-ip-address 203.0.113.5

# Create a dedicated SQL pool (data warehouse), DW100c = 100 DWUs
az synapse sql pool create \
  --workspace-name mySynapseWS \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDW \
  --performance-level DW100c

# Pause the dedicated pool to stop billing (data preserved)
az synapse sql pool pause \
  --workspace-name mySynapseWS \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDW

# Create an Apache Spark pool (2-8 nodes, auto-pause after 15 minutes)
az synapse spark pool create \
  --workspace-name mySynapseWS \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySpark \
  --spark-version 3.4 \
  --node-count 3 \
  --node-size Medium \
  --enable-auto-scale true \
  --min-node-count 2 \
  --max-node-count 8 \
  --delay 15

# Query ADLS Gen2 files via Serverless SQL (no infrastructure to manage)
# In Synapse Studio SQL editor:
# SELECT TOP 10 * FROM OPENROWSET(
#   BULK 'https://mystorageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net/rawdata/2024/01/**',
#   FORMAT = 'PARQUET'
# ) AS [result]

Azure Data Explorer is a fast, fully managed analytics service optimized for near-real-time exploration of large volumes of time-series data using the Kusto Query Language (KQL). ADX is the engine behind Azure Monitor Logs (Log Analytics), Application Insights, and Microsoft Sentinel. You can also provision standalone ADX clusters to analyze your own telemetry, IoT data, operational logs, and security event data.

# Create an ADX cluster (Developer SKU for dev/test)
az kusto cluster create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myADXCluster \
  --location eastus \
  --sku name=Dev_NoSLA_Standard_D11_v2 tier=Basic \
  --no-wait

# Create a database with 30-day hot cache and 365-day total retention
az kusto database create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myADXCluster \
  --database-name telemetryDB \
  --read-write-database hot-cache-period=P30D soft-delete-period=P365D location=eastus

# Create an Event Hub data connection (continuous ingestion from Event Hub)
az kusto data-connection event-hub create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myADXCluster \
  --database-name telemetryDB \
  --data-connection-name evhConn \
  --consumer-group eventConsumerGroup \
  --event-hub-resource-id $(az eventhubs eventhub show -g myRG --namespace-name myEventHubsNS -n telemetry --query id -o tsv) \
  --data-format JSON \
  --mapping-rule-name telemetryMapping \
  --location eastus

// KQL examples — analyze telemetry data in ADX
// Count events per device in the last hour
telemetry
| where ingestion_time() > ago(1h)
| summarize event_count = count() by deviceId
| order by event_count desc
| take 20

// 5-minute average temperature by device, detect anomalies
telemetry
| where timestamp > ago(24h)
| make-series avg_temp = avg(temperature) on timestamp from ago(24h) to now() step 5m by deviceId
| extend anomalies = series_decompose_anomalies(avg_temp, 1.5)
| mv-expand timestamp, avg_temp, anomalies
| where tolong(anomalies) != 0
| project timestamp, deviceId, avg_temp, anomalies

// Join two tables: devices metadata + telemetry
devices
| join kind=inner (
    telemetry | where timestamp > ago(1h) | summarize avg_temp = avg(temperature) by deviceId
) on deviceId
| project deviceId, location, manufacturer, avg_temp
| order by avg_temp desc

Azure Databricks is a first-party Microsoft service built on the Databricks Lakehouse Platform, optimized for Apache Spark workloads. Unlike Synapse Spark, Azure Databricks includes Delta Lake (an open-source storage format that adds ACID transactions and time-travel to Parquet files in ADLS Gen2), MLflow (experiment tracking, model registry), Unity Catalog (data governance across workspaces), and Photon (a native vectorized query engine that speeds up Spark SQL 2-20x).

Azure Databricks clusters are elastic: they auto-scale and auto-terminate when idle. The Databricks workspace provides notebooks (Python, Scala, SQL, R), a visual job scheduler, SQL warehouses for BI workloads, and Delta Live Tables for streaming ETL pipelines with automatic data quality constraints.

# Create an Azure Databricks workspace (Premium tier for Unity Catalog and MLflow)
az databricks workspace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDatabricks \
  --location eastus \
  --sku premium \
  --managed-resource-group myDatabricks-managed

# Get the workspace URL
az databricks workspace show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDatabricks \
  --query workspaceUrl \
  --output tsv

# Grant a user access to the Databricks workspace via Azure RBAC
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --assignee user@contoso.com \
  --scope $(az databricks workspace show -g myRG -n myDatabricks --query id -o tsv)

# Delta Lake operations in Databricks (PySpark)
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('delta-demo').getOrCreate()

# Write data to Delta Lake format
df = spark.read.parquet('abfss://raw@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net/events/2024/')
df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('abfss://silver@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net/events/')

# UPSERT (merge) new records into existing Delta table
delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'abfss://silver@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net/events/')

new_events = spark.read.json('abfss://raw@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net/events/today/')

delta_table.alias('target').merge(
    new_events.alias('source'),
    'target.eventId = source.eventId'
).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()

# Time-travel: read the table as it was 7 days ago
historical_df = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .option('timestampAsOf', '2026-06-23') \
    .load('abfss://silver@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net/events/')

# Vacuum old versions (keep 7 days of history by default)
delta_table.vacuum(retentionHours=168)

# OPTIMIZE: compact small files and build Z-order index on frequently filtered columns
spark.sql(
    'OPTIMIZE delta.`abfss://silver@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net/events/`'
    ' ZORDER BY (deviceId, timestamp)'
)
Batch ETL pipeline: copy from SQL Server to ADLS Gen2 daily
Real-time telemetry ingestion at millions of events/second
Stream processing: detect anomalies in sensor data in seconds
Ad-hoc SQL queries on Parquet files in ADLS Gen2 without loading data
Enterprise BI with complex SQL aggregations over 10-100 TB warehouse
Data engineering pipelines with Spark, Delta Lake, ML in the same platform
Log and telemetry analytics with KQL, near-real-time dashboards
Interactive ML development and training at scale
Real-time Power BI dashboard from streaming data
Stream Analytics with Power BI output
ELT orchestration within a Synapse workspace

Analytics pipelines are multi-service chains where a failure in any stage can silently stop data from reaching downstream consumers. Azure Monitor, ADF monitoring, and Event Hubs metrics are the primary tools for detecting and diagnosing failures.

# Monitor ADF pipeline run failures (alert on failed runs)
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name ADFPipelineFailureAlert \
  --scopes $(az datafactory factory show -g myRG -n myADF --query id -o tsv) \
  --condition 'total PipelineFailedRuns > 0' \
  --window-size 5m \
  --evaluation-frequency 1m \
  --severity 1 \
  --action-group $(az monitor action-group show -g myRG -n myActionGroup --query id -o tsv)

# Check Event Hubs incoming/outgoing message counts (health check)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource $(az eventhubs namespace show -g myRG -n myEventHubsNS --query id -o tsv) \
  --metric 'IncomingMessages' 'OutgoingMessages' 'ThrottledRequests' \
  --interval PT5M \
  --start-time "$(date -u -d '-1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --end-time "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --output table

# Check Stream Analytics job health metrics
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource $(az stream-analytics job show -g myRG -n myStreamJob --query id -o tsv) \
  --metric 'InputEvents' 'OutputEvents' 'InputEventSourcesBacklogged' 'Errors' \
  --interval PT5M \
  --start-time "$(date -u -d '-1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --end-time "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --output table

# If InputEventSourcesBacklogged > 0, the job is falling behind the stream
# Increase Streaming Units or optimize the query

# View ADF activity run details for a specific pipeline run
az datafactory activity-run query-by-pipeline-run \
  --factory-name myADF \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --run-id <RUN_ID> \
  --last-updated-after '2026-06-01T00:00:00Z' \
  --last-updated-before '2026-06-30T23:59:59Z' \
  --query 'value[].{activity:activityName, status:status, duration:durationInMs, error:error.message}' \
  --output table

Azure's analytics platform spans ingestion, storage, processing, serving, and visualization. The services are composable — you choose the right tool at each stage based on your latency requirements, data volume, and team skills.
Azure Data Factory is the ETL/ELT orchestration service for batch pipelines: 90+ connectors, schedule-triggered pipelines, Copy Activity for data movement, and Data Flows for visual transformation.
Azure Event Hubs ingests millions of events per second with Kafka protocol compatibility and optional Capture to automatically archive events to ADLS Gen2 or Blob Storage.
Azure Stream Analytics applies SQL windowing queries to real-time event streams with latency in seconds, outputting to SQL, Cosmos DB, Power BI, or Event Hubs.
Azure Synapse Analytics unifies SQL data warehouse (Dedicated SQL Pool), serverless SQL file querying, Apache Spark, and pipeline orchestration in one platform.

Azure Data Explorer is the KQL-based engine for time-series and telemetry analytics — it powers Log Analytics, Sentinel, and Application Insights, and can be provisioned as a standalone cluster for custom analytics.
